own_review.issue: False-positive leak reporting: ResultSets auto-closed by a user's proper Statement.close() (standard JDBC behavior) are counted via addLeakedResultSets() and reported as 'JDBC resources leaked' at connection close. The test change codifies this: statement.close() is added yet the leak warning is still asserted. Previously a closed statement removed itself and no leak was reported.

Exception swallowing skips close(): if next.isClosed() throws (StatementWrapper.isClosed delegates to the driver and can throw SQLException; partially-constructed wrappers can NPE), the catch swallows it and next.close() is never invoked - the resource is leaked by the very mechanism meant to prevent leaks, and it is undercounted.

Unsynchronized counter: plain int with non-atomic += and no volatile; statement closes on app threads race with connection close / leak reporting on transaction-completion or housekeeping threads - lost updates and visibility issues. Should be AtomicInteger or field-updater.

Memory retention: closed wrappers never unlink from the list. A long-held connection creating many statements accumulates unbounded StatementWrapper nodes in trackedStatements until connection close (previously wrappers removed themselves via releaseTrackedStatement/releaseTrackedResultSet). Same pattern for ResultSet wrappers per statement and ConnectionWrappers per transaction.
Garbled warning message: 'Closing open connection(s) on after completion' ('on after' typo).
Warning now fired once per batch instead of once per open connection - minor diagnostic information loss.

points.point: leakedResultSets plain int, non-atomic += - lost updates under concurrent statement closes; use AtomicInteger
points.note: Verified real; matches own finding #3, though it understates the cross-thread visibility aspect.

points.point: resultSetLeak test change shifts what is tested; original 'leaked Statement + ResultSets closed on connection close' path no longer exercised; suggest separate test
points.note: Real coverage regression, verified. However it wrongly endorses the warning assertion as 'still valid', missing that the warning is now a false positive.

points.point: closeTrackedStatements may be missing the trackedStatements null guard / else branch - 'please confirm'
points.note: The guard IS plainly present in the diff (if trackedStatements != null ... return JDBC_RESOURCES_NOT_LEAKED). Hedged absence-fabrication.

points.point: isClosed() override contract observation, 'no change needed', suggest documenting dual duty
ResultSetWrapper.close() removal of releaseTrackedResultSet is intentional and correct - narration
transactionBeforeCompletion count not included in warning message - diagnostics suggestion

missed: False-positive leak reporting for ResultSets auto-closed by Statement.close() (own #1) - point 2 touches the test but explicitly calls the assertion valid
Exception swallowing in closeAllAutocloseableElements causing close() to be skipped when isClosed() throws (own #2)
Memory retention: closed wrappers never unlink, unbounded growth on long-held connections (own #4)
Human: document AutoCloseableElement logic/invariants/ordering (no equivalent point)
exceeds: Test coverage regression in resultSetLeak (beyond human reference)

points.point: leakedResultSets plain int, non-atomic += under concurrent statement closes; use AtomicInteger/field-updater
points.note: Verified real; matches own finding #3. Minor citation slip (names removed releaseTrackedResultSet instead of closeTrackedResultSets).

points.point: Constructor publishes this via CAS before subclass fields initialized; concurrent traversal can see null fields, NPE swallowed and element skipped
points.note: Verified: super(head) links this before handler/wrappedStatement assigned; isClosed() dereferences them; swallowed exception skips close(). Real this-escape acknowledged by @SuppressWarnings.

points.point: Race between CAS insertion and traversal: element inserted around head reset missed by in-flight traversal, undercount at a given close, caught on next traversal; clarify javadoc
points.note: Verified from the CAS protocol; real subtle semantic gap (for enlistedOpenWrappers at transactionEnd the 'next traversal' may not come until handler reuse).

points.point: transactionEnd exception contract silently narrowed: old code propagated SQLException from wrapper.close(), new closeAllAutocloseableElements swallows all
points.note: Verified real behavior change; partially covers the exception-swallowing defect (own #2).

points.point: closeTrackedStatements simplification and releaseTrackedStatement removal narration - 'clean simplification'

missed: False-positive leak reporting for ResultSets auto-closed by Statement.close(), codified by the test change (own #1)
Memory retention: closed wrappers never unlink, unbounded growth on long-held connections (own #4)
exceeds: this-escape / partially-constructed wrapper NPE in traversal

qualitative: v7 clearly outperforms v5 on this concurrency-heavy PR: all four of its substantive points are verified real (non-atomic leakedResultSets, this-escape with swallowed NPE skipping close, insertion/traversal race undercount, silently narrowed exception contract), it commits one zero fabrications, and its AutoCloseableElement documentation requests substantially cover the human reviewer's sole point. v5 lands the leakedResultSets issue and a genuine test-coverage regression but burns credit with a hedged absence-fabrication about the closeTrackedStatements null guard that is plainly present in the diff, plus three narration-only points. Both candidates miss the PR's most user-visible defect - the leak warning now falsely fires for ResultSets properly auto-closed by Statement.close(), which the modified test codifies - and both miss the memory retention from closed wrappers never unlinking; v5 even endorses the test's warning assertion as valid. Net: v7 +2, v5 -3.
